title: Simple Plywood Tool Box
summary: A five-piece plywood tool box with a dowel-rod carry handle, sized to come entirely from one half-sheet of plywood.
description:
This is a good first build for someone learning hand tools — five flat pieces, straight cuts, and a handle, with nothing that requires a jig or a power router. The whole box comes from a single plywood half-sheet, which keeps material cost and waste both low.

The dowel-rod handle is nailed through both end pieces **with two nails per side**, not one. A single nail lets the handle rotate under load — exactly the failure mode you'd expect from something meant to be picked up and carried repeatedly.

Size the five pieces to fit your own half-sheet with a little room for saw kerf; there's no need to match another builder's panel layout exactly as long as the bottom, two sides, and two ends all fit together square.

tools.note: Or a circular saw for the straight panel cuts.

steps.title: Lay out and cut the five pieces
steps.body: Lay out a bottom, two sides and two ends on the 18in x 26in half-sheet, and settle the relationships before you settle the sizes, because they determine every cut. The two sides lap the bottom's long edges, so each side is 1" longer than the bottom (1/2" of end panel at each end) and the finished box is 1" wider than the bottom; the two ends then fit between the sides, exactly as wide as the bottom. Cut the two end panels taller than the sides — they have to stand above the box to carry the handle, and nothing else in the build does that job. Draw the whole layout on the panel first and leave a saw kerf between every piece, then cut all five.

steps.title: Nail the sides to the bottom
steps.body: Stand a side panel against one long edge of the bottom, with the 1/2" overhang showing equally at each end and flush along the underside, and nail through the side into the bottom's edge. Do the same on the other long edge. Nailing into the 1/2" plywood edge is the weakest joint in the box, so space the nails every couple of inches, keep them centered on the bottom's thickness, and add glue — a nail that drifts high or low breaks out through a face. Check that the two sides stay parallel as you work; if they splay, the end panels will not drop in square.

steps.title: Close the box with the end panels
steps.body: Drop the two end panels in between the sides, sitting on the bottom and flush with the ends of the side panels, and nail through the sides into the end panels' edges. Their tall portions should stand up past the sides by the same amount on both ends, since that is what the handle bridges. Check the box for square across its diagonals before the last nails go in — once both ends are fastened the box is fixed, and a racked tool box will rock on a bench forever.

steps.title: Add the handle
steps.body: Bore a 1-1/4" hole through each end panel at the same height above the sides, marking both off the same reference edge so the handle sits level. Back each hole with scrap on the exit side so the plywood does not blow out. Cut the dowel long enough to reach the outside face of both ends, tap it through, and nail through each end panel into the dowel with two nails per side — two, not one, because a single nail lets the handle spin in its hole under load, which is exactly how a carry handle fails on something meant to be picked up over and over.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand every surface and edge, paying particular attention to the top edges of the sides and the handle itself since those are the parts hands touch. Ease the outside corners so the box does not gouge whatever it gets set down on. Then paint or finish; sealing the plywood's exposed edge grain matters more than the faces, because that is where a shop tool box takes on moisture and starts to swell.
